concepts.title: Electric Potential
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Potential  

"Potential is the potential energy per unit charge." Electric potential at any point in an electric field is defined as the potential energy per unit charge, same as field strength is defined as the force per unit charge.

Thus $\text{V}=\frac{\text{U}}{{{\text{q}}_{0}}}$ or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ U}={{\text{q}}_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}$

The SI unit of potential is Volt (V) which is Joule per Coloumb. So 1 Volt $=1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ J}/\text{C}$ If we want a picture of a field, we usually think of many lines lines of "flux" emanating radially away from the charge :

For negative charges, the lines point inward. These flux lines indicate the direction of a positively charged test particle would move due to the electric force between it and the configuration charge. Note that the "density per unit area" of the flux decreases as the distance from the center increases. That is, the lines are much closer together when nearer to their origin. Since we live in three spatial dimensions, and the surface area of a sphere centered on the charge increases with the square of the distance, we have a model for the inverse square force law : the magnitude of the force is proportional to the density of the field lines ("flux density"), which decreases with the square of the distance. We can also see why spherical source is equivalent to a point source at its center : the flux lines emanating from the surface can be extrapolated inwards to the center with no change in the physical forces felt outside the surface.

The electric field can be thought of as the gradient of the electrical potential field. But since the electric field is a force per unit charge, the electric potential must be an energy per unit charge. It is a scalar field, having a magnitude (but no direction) at every point in space.

$\text{V}\left( \text{r} \right)=\frac{\text{q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\text{r}}$

also

$\text{U}={{\text{q}}_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}$

As with the cases of the electric field and force, the electrical potential field superposes lineraly, so that the field at a point due to a collection of configuration charges is equal to the sum of the fields due to the individual charges, at that point. For example, if in a vacuum two protons $\left( {{\text{q}}_{\text{c}1}} \right)$ lie in the xy plane at $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)=\left( -2,1 \right)$ Angstroms and an electron $\left( {{\text{q}}_{\text{c}2}} \right)$ lies at the origin $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)=\left( 0,0 \right)$, the energy of an electron $\left( {{\text{q}}_{\text{t}}} \right)$ at $\left( {{\text{x}}_{\text{t}}},{{\text{y}}_{\text{t}}} \right)=\left( 1,1 \right)$ Angstroms would be

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{U} & ~={{\text{U}}_{\text{tc}1}}+{{\text{U}}_{\text{tc}2}} \\ {} & \left. ={{\text{q}}_{\text{t}}}\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{c}1}}+{{\text{V}}_{\text{c}2}} \right) \right) \\ {} & ~=\frac{\left( -\text{e} \right)\left( 2\text{e} \right)}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\times 3\times {{10}^{-10}}}+\frac{\left( -\text{e} \right)\left( \text{e} \right)}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\sqrt{2}\times {{10}^{-10}}} \\ {} & ~=9.33\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{10}^{-20}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ J}, \\ \end{array}$

where the distances between charges are

$\text{r}=\sqrt{{{\left( {{\text{x}}_{\text{t}}}-{{\text{x}}_{\text{i}}} \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( {{\text{y}}_{\text{t}}}-{{\text{y}}_{\text{i}}} \right)}^{2}}}$

and "i" denotes either of the configuration charges.

concepts.title: Relation Between Electric Field and Potential
concepts.conceptBody:
Relation between Electric Potential and Electric Field 

In order to compute the direction of the force felt by a test charge in a potential field, one can take the gradient of the potential

${{\mathbf{E}}_{\text{x}}}=-\frac{\delta \mathbf{V}}{\delta \text{x}}$

If we were to plot the electrical potential field due to a pair of configuration charges, we would see that there are lines, called "equipotentials", on which the electrical potential has the same value everywhere :

The direction of the gradient (and hence the electric field) is perpendicular to the equipotential lines since its component along (tangent to) the equipotentials is zero ( $\text{V}$ is a constant). We can therefore immediately trace the path of any test particle in the field, since charges move in the direction of least energy.

Absolute Potential at Some Point 

Suppose we take the point $b$ at infinity and as a reference point assign the value ${{V}_{b}}=0$, the equations can be written as

${{\text{V}}_{\text{a}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{b}}}=\frac{{{\left( {{\text{W}}_{\text{a}-\text{b}}} \right)}_{\text{electric }\text{ force }}}}{{{\text{q}}_{0}}}=\frac{{{\left( {{\text{W}}_{\text{b}-\text{a}}} \right)}_{\text{external }\text{ force }}}}{{{\text{q}}_{0}}}$

or ${{\text{V}}_{\text{a}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{b}}}=\frac{{{\left( {{\text{W}}_{\text{a}-\infty }} \right)}_{\text{electric }\text{ force }}}}{{{\text{q}}_{0}}}=\frac{{{\left( {{\text{W}}_{\infty -\text{a}}} \right)}_{\text{external }\text{ force }}}}{{{\text{q}}_{0}}}$

Thus, the absolute electric potential at point a in an electric field can be defined as the work done in displacing a unit positive charge from infinity to a by the external force or the work done per unit positive charge in displacing it from a to infinity.

Note : Following three formulae are very useful in the problems related to work done in electric field.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & {{\left( {{\text{W}}_{\text{a}-\text{b}}} \right)}_{\text{electric }\text{ force }}}={{\text{q}}_{0}}\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{a}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{b}}} \right) \\ {} & \left( {{\text{W}}_{\text{a}-\text{b}}} \right)\text{ external }\text{ force }={{\text{q}}_{0}}\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{b}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{a}}} \right)=-\left( {{\text{W}}_{\text{a}-\text{b}}} \right)\text{ electric }\text{ force } \\ {} & {{\left( {{\text{W}}_{\infty -\text{a}}} \right)}_{\text{external }\text{ force }}}={{\text{q}}_{0}}{{\text{V}}_{\text{a}}} \\ \end{array}$

Here, ${{\text{q}}_{0}}$, and ${{\text{V}}_{\text{b}}}$ are to be substituted with sign.

Note : Here we can see that the electron (a negative charge) moves from B (lower potential) to A (higher potential) and the work done by electric force is positive. Therefore, we may conclude that whenever a negative charge moves from a lower potential to higher potential work done by the electric force is positive or when a positive charge moves from lower potential to higher potential the work done by the electric force is negative.

concepts.title: Electric Potential Due to Point Charge
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Potential Due to a Point Charge $q$

Work done against the field in slow moving a unit positive charge from infinity to the point in the field is called electric potential at that point.

From the definition of potential, $V=\frac{U}{{{q}_{0}}}=\frac{\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{{{q}_{{{q}_{0}}}}}{r}}{{{q}_{0}}}$

$\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{r}}$

Here, $r$ is the distance from the point charge $q$ to the point at which the potential is evaluated. If $\text{q}$ is positive, the potential that it produces is positive at all points; if $\text{q}$ is negative, it produces a potential that is negative everywhere. In either case, $\text{V}$ is equal to zero at $\text{r}=\infty $.

concepts.title: Electric Potential Due to System of Charges
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Potential Due to a System of Charges 

Just as the electric field due to a collection of point charges is the vector sum of the fields produced by each charge, the electric potential due to a collection of point charge is the sclar sum of the potentials due to each charge.

$\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\underset{\text{i}}{\mathop \sum }\,\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{i}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{i}}}}$

In this expression, ${{r}_{i}}$ is the distance from the ${{i}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ charge, ${{q}_{i}}$, to the point at which $V$ is evaluated. For a continuous distribution of charge along a line, over a surface, or through a volume, we divide the charge into elements $\text{dq}$ and the sum in the above equation becomes an integral,

$\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\int ~\frac{\text{dq}}{\text{r}}$

Note : In the equation $\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}{{\sum }_{\text{i}}}\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{i}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{i}}}}$ or $\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\int \frac{\text{dq}}{\text{r}}$ if the whole charge is at equal distance ${{\text{r}}_{0}}$ from the point where $\text{V}$ is to be evaluated, then we can write

$\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}{{{\text{r}}_{0}}}$

Where ${{q}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ is the algebraic sum of all the charges of which the system is made.

concepts.title: Equipotential Surface
concepts.conceptBody:
Equipotential Surface

The equipotential surfaces in an electric field have the same basic idea as topographic maps used by civil engineers or mountain climbers. On a topographic map, contour lines are drawn passing through the points having the same elevation. The potential energy of a mass $m$ does not change along a contour line as the elevation is same everywhere. An equipotential surface is a three dimensional surface on which the electrical potential $\text{V}$ is the same at every point on it. An equipotential surface has the following characteristics.

  1. Potential difference between any two points in an equipotential surface is zero.
  2. If a test charge ${{\text{q}}_{0}}$ is moved from one point to the other on such a surface, the electric potential energy ${{\text{q}}_{0}}\text{ }\text{ V}$ remains constant.
  3. No work is done by the electric force when the test charge is moved along this surface.
  4. Two equipotential surfaces can never intersect each other because otherwise the point of intersection will have two potentials which is of course not acceptable.
  5. As the work done by electric force is zero when a test charge is moved along the equipotential surface, it follows that $\vec{E}$ must be perpendicular to the surface at every point so that the electric force ${{\text{q}}_{0}}\vec{E}$ will always be perpendicular to the displacement of a charge moving on the surface.

Thus, field lines and equipotential surfaces are always mutually perpendicular. Some equipotential surfaces are shown below : 
 

Equipotential surfaces are always perpendicular to field lines.

(i) When field lines are parallel, equipotential surface are planar sheets $\left( {{\text{V}}_{1}}>{{\text{V}}_{2}}>{{\text{V}}_{3}} \right)$

(ii) For a point charge, equipotential surface are concentric spherical surfaces centred at q. $\left( {{\text{V}}_{1}}>{{\text{V}}_{2}}>{{\text{V}}_{3}} \right.$ when $\left. \text{q}<0 \right)$

Note : Equipotential surface is locus of points where potential remain constant. To find the field intensity when Equipotential Surface are given
